essays.title: Why Time Feels So Weird Right Now
essays.author: Alan Burdick
essays.hook: Our subjective sense of time is elastic — stress, novelty, and routine stretch and compress it in ways neuroscience is only beginning to map.

essays.title: The Art of Being Still
essays.author: Pico Iyer
essays.hook: In an age of speed, nothing is more invigorating than going slow — and nothing more urgent than sitting still.

essays.title: How to Do Nothing
essays.author: Jenny Odell
essays.hook: Doing nothing is hard. In a world where our value is determined by productivity, choosing to do nothing is a political act.

essays.title: The Busy Trap
essays.author: Tim Kreider
essays.hook: If you live in America and you're not busy, you're somehow deficient. But busyness serves as a kind of existential reassurance.

essays.title: Consider the Lobster
essays.author: David Foster Wallace
essays.hook: Is it all right to boil a sentient creature alive just for our gustatory pleasure? A philosophical inquiry from a food festival.

essays.title: This Is Water
essays.author: David Foster Wallace
essays.hook: The capital-T Truth is about life before death — about making it to 30, or maybe 50, without wanting to shoot yourself in the head.

essays.title: The Egg
essays.author: Andy Weir
essays.hook: Every time you victimized someone, you were victimizing yourself. Every act of kindness you've done, you've done to yourself.

essays.title: Solitude and Leadership
essays.author: William Deresiewicz
essays.hook: If you want others to follow, learn to be alone with your thoughts.

essays.title: The Cook and the Chef: Musk's Secret Sauce
essays.author: Tim Urban
essays.hook: Most people are chefs (following recipes). The rare few are cooks (reasoning from first principles).

essays.title: 1000 True Fans
essays.author: Kevin Kelly
essays.hook: To be a successful creator you don't need millions of fans. You need only 1,000 true fans.

essays.title: Teach Yourself Programming in Ten Years
essays.author: Peter Norvig
essays.hook: Why is everyone in such a rush? Walk into any bookshop and you'll see 'Learn Java in 24 Hours.' The key is deliberate practice over years.

essays.title: The Tail End
essays.author: Tim Urban
essays.hook: When you look at your life in terms of remaining experiences rather than remaining years, certain things become startlingly clear.

essays.title: You and Your Research
essays.author: Richard Hamming
essays.hook: Why do so few scientists make significant contributions while so many are forgotten? The difference is courage and focus.

essays.title: Politics and the English Language
essays.author: George Orwell
essays.hook: Political language is designed to make lies sound truthful. But the slovenliness of our language makes it easier for us to have foolish thoughts.

essays.title: The Pleasure of Finding Things Out
essays.author: Richard Feynman
essays.hook: I was born not knowing and have had only a little time to change that here and there.

essays.title: What I Talk About When I Talk About Running
essays.author: Haruki Murakami
essays.hook: Pain is inevitable. Suffering is optional. Running taught me everything about writing.

essays.title: How Will You Measure Your Life?
essays.author: Clayton M. Christensen
essays.hook: The same theories that help companies avoid disruption can help individuals lead meaningful lives.

essays.title: A Mathematician's Lament
essays.author: Paul Lockhart
essays.hook: If I had to design a mechanism for the express purpose of destroying a child's natural curiosity about mathematics, I couldn't do a better job than school.

essays.title: The Crossroads of Should and Must
essays.author: Elle Luna
essays.hook: Should is how others want us to live. Must is who we are and what we believe. It's our convictions, our calling.

essays.title: What You'll Wish You'd Known
essays.author: Paul Graham
essays.hook: Don't decide too soon. The most important thing is to stay upwind — work on hard problems.

essays.title: Walking
essays.author: Henry David Thoreau
essays.hook: I think that I cannot preserve my health and spirits unless I spend four hours a day at least sauntering through the woods.

essays.title: Self-Reliance
essays.author: Ralph Waldo Emerson
essays.hook: Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string. A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds.

essays.title: The Inner Ring
essays.author: C.S. Lewis
essays.hook: The quest to be inside the invisible inner ring will break your heart unless you break free of it.

essays.title: Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule
essays.author: Paul Graham
essays.hook: There are two types of schedule. A single meeting can blow a whole afternoon for someone on the maker's schedule.

essays.title: The Days Are Long But The Decades Are Short
essays.author: Sam Altman
essays.hook: 36 pieces of life advice on turning 30 — compound yourself, be willing to look stupid, and don't let yourself get pushed around.

stories.title: An Occurrence at Owl Creek Bridge
stories.author: Ambrose Bierce

stories.title: A Good Man is Hard to Find
stories.author: Flannery O'Connor

stories.title: The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County
stories.author: Mark Twain

stories.title: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
stories.author: James Thurber

stories.title: The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas
stories.author: Ursula K. Le Guin

life_lessons.maxim: The obstacle is the way. What stands in your path becomes your path — the struggle itself is where skill, patience, and character are forged, not merely tested.
life_lessons.reflection: What's one difficulty you're currently resisting that might actually be the most productive thing you could lean into?

life_lessons.maxim: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act but a habit.
life_lessons.reflection: What is one small action you could do daily that would compound into something meaningful over a year?

life_lessons.maxim: The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best time is now.
life_lessons.reflection: What have you been postponing because it feels 'too late' to start?

life_lessons.maxim: You can't connect the dots looking forward; you can only connect them looking backward. So you have to trust that the dots will somehow connect in your future.
life_lessons.reflection: When has a seemingly random experience later turned out to be exactly what you needed?

life_lessons.maxim: Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response. In our response lies our growth and our freedom.
life_lessons.source: Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning
life_lessons.reflection: When did you last react automatically to something that deserved a pause first?

life_lessons.maxim: The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones.
life_lessons.reflection: What large goal could you break into a single 15-minute action today?

life_lessons.maxim: If you are depressed, you are living in the past. If you are anxious, you are living in the future. If you are at peace, you are living in the present.
life_lessons.reflection: Where is your mind spending most of its time right now — past, future, or present?

life_lessons.maxim: The only true wisdom is in knowing you know nothing.
life_lessons.reflection: In what area of your life are you most confident — and could that confidence be hiding a blind spot?

life_lessons.maxim: Compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world. He who understands it, earns it; he who doesn't, pays it.
life_lessons.reflection: Where in your life — finances, relationships, skills — are you benefiting from compounding, and where are you paying compound costs?

life_lessons.maxim: The purpose of life is not to be happy. It is to be useful, to be honourable, to be compassionate, to have it make some difference that you have lived.
life_lessons.reflection: If you measured today not by happiness but by usefulness, how would you rate it?

life_lessons.maxim: Memento mori — remember you will die. Not as morbidity, but as clarity. Knowing time is finite makes each choice weigh what it should.
life_lessons.reflection: If you had five years left, what would you stop doing immediately?

life_lessons.maxim: Ships in harbour are safe, but that's not what ships are built for.
life_lessons.reflection: What is one 'safe' choice you're making that's actually holding you back from your purpose?

life_lessons.maxim: You do not rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems.
life_lessons.reflection: What system (not goal) could you design this week that would make progress automatic?

life_lessons.maxim: No man ever steps in the same river twice, for it's not the same river and he's not the same man.
life_lessons.reflection: How have you changed in the last year in ways you haven't fully acknowledged?

life_lessons.maxim: The greatest enemy of knowledge is not ignorance; it is the illusion of knowledge.
life_lessons.reflection: What do you 'know' that you've never actually tested or verified?

life_lessons.maxim: How you do anything is how you do everything. Small disciplines repeated with consistency lead to great achievements gained slowly over time.
life_lessons.reflection: What 'small' thing are you doing carelessly that might be a signal about your larger patterns?

life_lessons.maxim: The cost of a thing is the amount of life you exchange for it.
life_lessons.reflection: Is there a regular expense (time or money) in your life that isn't worth the life-hours it costs?

life_lessons.maxim: Luck is what happens when preparation meets opportunity. Position yourself at the intersection.
life_lessons.reflection: What are you preparing for right now — and are you putting yourself in places where opportunity can find you?

life_lessons.maxim: Everyone thinks of changing the world, but no one thinks of changing himself.
life_lessons.reflection: What change are you hoping to see in others or in circumstances that you could first model yourself?

life_lessons.maxim: The two most important days in your life are the day you are born and the day you find out why.
life_lessons.reflection: If you had to articulate your 'why' in one sentence today, what would it be?

life_lessons.maxim: Comparison is the thief of joy. Run your own race at your own pace — the only meaningful benchmark is yesterday's version of yourself.
life_lessons.reflection: Whose progress have you been comparing yourself to — and what would change if you stopped?

life_lessons.maxim: What gets measured gets managed. But be careful what you measure — you'll optimise for it whether you meant to or not.
life_lessons.reflection: What metric are you unconsciously optimising for in your daily life? Is it the right one?

life_lessons.maxim: The reading of all good books is like a conversation with the finest minds of past centuries.
life_lessons.reflection: When did you last let a book genuinely change your mind about something?

life_lessons.maxim: Silence is a source of great strength. In the space of not-speaking, you hear what matters.
life_lessons.reflection: When did you last sit in complete silence for ten minutes — and what surfaced?

life_lessons.maxim: The only way to do great work is to love what you do. If you haven't found it yet, keep looking. Don't settle.
life_lessons.reflection: What part of your current work do you genuinely love — and how could you do more of it?
